item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ROUNDERHOUSE: proving that reading can be fun for a person with ADHD if you have the right formatting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the first Sin?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fairly predictable “Member of the foundation abuses D-class/anomalies, gets punished” type of story. Which would be okay, but the emotional beats didn’t hit me. I think it’s weaker for the fact that 7 and sharpe are so clearly in the wrong that I can’t relate to their POV at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it, Rog!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The relationship between D-293 and Boucher is asked to carry too much weight here, since the other characters don't do as much to pick up the slack. I don't think the use of a cliched version of the Foundation dealing in well-worn tropes is a bad thing, or even that it's necessarily done badly here. But there is a lot here that we've seen before in one way or another. I saw at least one other commentator bring up "The Thing" as a comparable monster and I think that the key difference here is that The Thing is a far more faceless abomination than what we have here, and a completely different type of story.

It was the right choice to focus more on the characters trying to find their way through a terrible situations it means that even though on some level every experienced reader knows they're going through the motions, but there are fresh faces to carry it. I know I said above that the characters I didn't name don't do as much to carry the weight of the story as Boucher and D-293, but that isn't to say they don't carry any of it at all.

Overall, I feel like this is a really strong execution done with some relatively weak pieces. Taken as a whole, it's greater than the sum of its parts and it's actually fairly impressive how well its done when you consider each individual piece in a vacuum. None of it would work without the rest, and that's an extremely tough needle to thread. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first half of this is such a complete mess I unwittingly tried to downvote a second time, I was legitimately shocked to see who had authored it.

+ show block
– hide block

I'm not going to touch on any supposed tired tropes mentioned by other readers. Though I agree to some extent, execution can work some marvelous wonders… however

The opening description is fine on first reading, but isn't congruent with the rest of the article. The only information we have about the anomaly is that it's "ontokinetic", but there's nothing concrete in the body of the article which would suggest anything about bending reality (besides some yammering about hume levels) she's exposed to mutagenic skips that alter her physicality and she hulks out. Also, why would the main point of contact for this anomaly remain someone who was stripped of their position?

Side note: In hindsight, since she doesn't go John Carpenter on his ass on camera at any point, it's odd to denote them as a singular anomalous entity.

No major issues with the first section of dialogue. Though it is one of the points that — as others have mentioned — ends up more as a monologue, Seven is colorful enough that I didn't particularly mind. It's also moving the story forward

I did have a hangup with Seven's bit of dialogue starting at "Just remember". Its odd for him to give what's essentially a warning about how the consequences could affect him before immediately stating it doesn't really matter, worry about yourself. It just reads strange in a way I can't articulate.

The project proposal really, really irked me. It's vague as to be effectively worthless, and I was left wondering why it would be such a standout submission to catch the attention of Seven. I understood at the time that the department was on its last legs… but had they been doing literally nothing such that a rando simply proposing a hazy crosstest for the Foundation's benefit is enough to jumpstart an entire department?

Our introduction to D may as well be contentless for all it contributes to the narrative. We're offered basic SCPF overview #100001 (and why would the head of research be doing this anyway? This didn't come up at any point before their meeting?). I don't have any sense of character from D besides them coming off as a bit of a dullard — which doesn't make her stand out in any way from the countless D's across as many articles who have the same schtick. (Again, as tropes are tools, this by itself isn't bad, but coupled with the other issues of this dialogue, there's no saving grace.)

An explanation of their particular undertaking would be a helluva lot more relevant here… speaking of relevancy: beyond the names of the characters, it fells like it could belong in almost any article.

This section could be cut and not a damn thing would change in the work and that's not good. This was where I downvoted. I almost stopped reading here.

First testing log has two major issues. Foremost: There's absolutely no reason for the second set of boxes, as there is no accompanying test, it's just providing further context for the first one. It seems like a huge formatting oversight to present it as it is.

(Sidenote: holy shit I love the ellipses crosslink infobox!)

Secondly, this is the very first anomaly used in the article for crosstesting aaaaand it's used in a way that's not even remotely in-line with how the object functions. It's stated that 494 can only be used on whatever the wearer can carry in their hand without any assistance or additional support — yet here the wearer can just place their hand on something to activate it?

The inconsistencies continue into the next log: how exactly are these effects related to (barely) trading some properties with titanium? I'm not sure where the leap from "metal shoulder" to "elongated neck and brittle bones" came from. It doesn't seem to be consistent; I'd thought the log was accidentally cut and pasted from elsewhere in the article from how poorly it flowed from what I'd just seen. (The following log, D only comments on a metal arm, saying nothing of her physical state remarked on here, which only makes this addendum seem like an odd inclusion overall.)

After this point the inconsistencies definitely drop off, but it was brutal getting to it. I would only nitpick on:

Growth did not halt after the limb had reformed.

It's unclear what this means. Is… it still growing to this day? Like, one uber-long leg?

and

Can I get you anything.

In all, the best part of this for me was being introduced to a new formatting tidbit. Besides the general conceit of an EC backstory, it doesn't have any redeeming qualities in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with a lot of the given criticism. There isn't really much here to make it stand out from similar stories and I kept waiting for something to pop out to differentiate it from others. I also dislike how the EC gave 07 a slap on the wrist and just dumped everything on Sharp. Boot licking is the last thing I expect of the EC.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strongly agree, loved the short description notes! Great innovation, hope it becomes a new standard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

The project goal is fuzzy and undefined, and this isn't really acknowledged until the review at the end, beyond the thin justification of O5-7 being a sentimental ass.

Most conversations feel one-sided. One character is in control and the other just asks questions or meekly as an obedient strawman for whatever the point of the conversation is. Characterisation falls apart so that a given character reacts how they're needed to.

The use of so many Series 1 articles made everything feel dated, slightly inaccessible. I felt like I was reading an 001 from several years ago. And the sheer number of tests becomes dull after a few, becoming filler between the real content content.

Sharp flip-flops back and forth between using different pronouns for 5549 - "she", "them" and "it" - which would be bad enough, let alone him being so corrective about it.

At no point is 5549 actually classified as 5549. You have this set of miscellaneous, unclassified experiments, and suddenly O5-7 comes out with "What about 5549?" during the tribunal. Other than the description at the start and the section headers this is the first mention of 5549 in the article.

Having read ARD's critique I'm inclined to agree with its core point, if not the details. There's no plot element introduced here that feels new to me; at no point was I surprised. I mean, there's even a spooky note at the end.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Olympia round 2? I like this one better. Don't quite understand why there are multiple voices at the end, though, or where the ontokinesis actually comes from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lets start with the criticisms. I thought the dialogue and characters were pretty bland, and sometimes felt weird to me. The story was the part I most enjoyed, but even then there were sections where the dialogue felt too story-driven, like the characters were… well, characters in a story, for lack of a better analogy. In particular, I think you could have done a lot more with the initial interviews with D-293, before testing got really underway, so we could have gotten a more established character and really connected with them instead of having what felt to me like a pretty standard copy+paste D-Class.

Now for the good - I thought the story was fun and moved fast enough that I didn't get bored while also being paced well enough that it wasn't weird and confusing. I think the character that stood out to me as the best written was Sharp, whose dialogue made him feel like a fleshed out villain with a real agenda. I think this SCP has its problems, but I think it's still something to be proud of. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a wonderful piece of writing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gave it a +1, because I enjoyed the read. But it seems like every new skip these days ends with a "(1) New Message" button, like, to an uncommon degree, and I just find that weird. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was superb, but I do agree that it can be fleshed out more. The short footnote descriptions were a nice touch and should be included in all cross-references. +1

item.title: Re: ddddadsdf
item.content:encoded: Echoing Rum's sentiments. It's definitely not bad, but there's some parts that feel sort of "eh." Novote here too.

item.title: Re: ddddadsdf
item.content:encoded: Same page here.

item.title: ddddadsdf
item.content:encoded:
Copy and pasted from a discord convo

I guess I should start out by saying I'm not a huge fan of "kaktusy" scps, which is the vibe I get from this- terminal stuff, format screw of descriptions and stuffbig focus on O5 personnel, the history of the Foundation, and reality bending anomalies

just feels overused to me

Your dialogue feels clunky

slang and swearing that's thrown in without a strong ear for how it sounds and just feels a bit disruptive, along with feeling a bit cliche, and it feels like it's going for a natural cadence but doesn't quite hit it. It doesn't feel like it moves like a real conversation

calling it bloody work and then ugly work a few lines later feels a bit rough

it feels like the characters are talking mostly for the reader's benefit, not for each other's

the D class feels far too cooperative

with no explanation that I can see [addendum: there is an explanation, but i don't find it satisfying to explain the passivity of the behavior]

the form of the story feels like one I've seen a dozen times, with the Foundation doing spooky dangerous testing and creating an anomaly

the characterization of the three main individuals doesn't feel particularly strong

Boucher is the nice one, Sharp is mean, the test subject is scared and going through a lot of shit, and there's some layers beneath those, but it still feels kind of flat

novote overall

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a neat idea – giving an in-universe explanation for the out-of-universe evolution in writing culture. Among the good choices, let me say that selecting just Series I scips for the experiments to evoke that time in the site's history was a nice touch.

Unfortunately, there were some details in the execution that bring me down to a no-vote.

A lot of elements felt a little implausible to me. The effects of some of the experiments didn't seem in keeping with (my understanding of) the scips involved. D-293 seemed weirdly stoical and forgiving of her captors even after she'd become a 2m tall, half-metal, hollow-boned freak and repeatedly brought close to death. The informal constitution of the Ethics Committee appears to bestow them just insane, absolute power – which as we all know corrupts absolutely. And the fact that the Foundation, even after it changed into its brand new new every-life-is-sacred uniform, decided to let Boucher stay kidnapped for months, possibly even until the present day, just seemed weird.

Then there was the characterisation. I only got hazy, cartoonish senses of who these people were, and the dialogue often felt like it had been cut down to the bone and then some. D-293 acting in that strange way is part of this.

And I agree with a much weaker version of ARD's claim, in that "humanoid that the Foundation tried to exploit busts out and kills some folks" did seem slightly cliché, although I will admit that the kidnapping added a little herby freshness to that base.

I think I'd have enjoyed this more if it were more fleshed out – the characterisation, the dialogue, the escape log. I'm just not a fan of the incredibly pared down, trim every ounce of fat, drop all unnecessary weight style of some of the most highly upvoted authors of the past couple of Series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree with ARD here, everything on the wiki is derived from something and even then, I saw no connections to 1730 and 3241. I haven’t read the other two to my knowledge so I can’t speak on those.

I liked what you were going for here for the most part though I think it all just unraveled very quickly. Tension was never really built up for me, I felt as if we went from test log immediately to test analysis to interview log without allowing me as a reader to ponder on the D-class’ situation. I think that’s in how short the dialogue is in the interview logs, I really didn’t see any characterization for the D-class that would allow me to sympathize with it further than thinking what the foundation is doing is wrong.

I did enjoy this as an origin story for the Ethics Committee however. I wish you wrote a bit more on their creation and their first trial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, but should "cliche" be spelled "cliché" or is that stylistic? Not editing just in case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah this is pretty much on the nose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think we're clearly reading from Maria Jones' perspective (logging in via retinal scan at first), and O5-14 is just one of her user accounts (the idea that there would be files that Maria Jones can't read doesn't make sense, but it makes sense that she might need to use a different account for level 6 clearance).

Why is she reading it? Because they offered her O5-7 and she's reading up on why there's an opening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it and love it the more i read it; good hook with a good payoff, but i still have some questions:
Fourteen is the Admin and we're reading this from their perspective for rememberance or in case they needed a little jog in their memory cause 293 is about to breach, but then we see them opening up Maria's account – is Maria Jones the Admin?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 'plot points' copied are nonexistent - the only similarity between this and Dead Men is the Ethics Committee getting one over on the Council. I haven't read 2419, and 1730 & 3241 have no meaningful concept that is also used in this article in the same way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I don't entirely disagree that there are elements of this piece that are done better elsewhere, I think if you're coming into this article actively trying to compare it to what's out there you're setting yourself up to not enjoy it. Like, its fine if this ruined the article for you, but it feels weird to me to say "see how all these other articles did it better?" rather than to look at the article in a vacuum.

I'm not saying there aren't legitimate criticisms that can be made of this article. What I am saying is that its not like… super useful to compare it to other ones. "not unique or compelling" is not exactly actionable critique, so while you're fine to dislike the article, I'm not sure what Rounder is going to like… do with this response.

ARD was a coward and edited his comment so this doesn't make sense any more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy to see this up at long last.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Copying multiple plot points, beat-for-beat, from multiple different articles and stringing them together into a single work is derivative. Two major tropes this article leans on heavily — almost exclusively — are 'Foundation fucks something up by cross-testing and repeated testing' and 'Foundation testing on D-Classes comes back to bite them'. But it fails to build on either trope in a unique or compelling way, which only exacerbates the issue of a predictable and uninspired plot. I’m particularly disappointed that the D-Class’s potential character arc simply ended with her turning into a monster and killing a lot of offscreen grunts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, if you have to point to four or five different articles to support a claim of it being derivative, that just means it casts a wide net of inspiration — Like, literally any story on this site can be boiled down to its individual plot points, and no plot point has ever just been used once. I'm fine with the second half of your criticism and I think that if this article's plot points matched one other article's beat for beat, you could say it was derivative. But as it is, having 4 different similarities with 4 different articles is far from derivative. Also - all your examples linked, save Dead Men, is just another article that uses the plot structure of `Foundation fucks something up`, which is like - barely even a trope? Certainly not enough to be a criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is big but derivative. All the main character moments and story beats have been hit in a bigger and ultimately bolder way by other articles: SCP-1730, SCP-2419, SCP-3241, and Dead Men, to name a few. There wasn't a single part of the article I didn't expect or not see coming, and the CSS wasn't stylish enough to excuse that fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took ya long enough, ya goof. Well worth the wait. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely superb.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: been waiting for this for so long i have abandoned drafts that crosslink to it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: took you long enough

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SECOND SIN

BY ROUNDERHOUSE

This has literally been in the oven for a year — admittedly on and off. But I wanted my fiftieth page on this site to be something special, and this is it. I literally cannot name all of the people who helped me with this labour of love, so you know who you are: thank you. A special shout out to Tufto, Kirby, and Nico for viewing this in damn near every iteration, and Xilas for haranguing me into finishing it. This was originally meant to be the flagship to a series of articles… but living in development hell means all the other articles came out before it. Thank you, and I hope you enjoyed!

ROUNDERPAGE

+ show block
– hide block

science.png — http://www.scp-wiki.net/list-of-foundation-s-internal-departments
seal.png — http://www.scp-wiki.net/component:the-way-out-theme
